The **municipalities of Brazil** ([Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language"): *municípios do Brasil*) are [administrative divisions](/wiki/Administrative_division "Administrative division") of the [Brazilian states](/wiki/States_of_Brazil "States of Brazil"). [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil") currently has 5,571 municipalities, which, given the 2025 population estimate of 213,421,037[[1]](#cite_note-1), makes an average municipality population of 37,728 inhabitants. The average state in Brazil has 214 municipalities. [Roraima](/wiki/Roraima "Roraima") is the least subdivided state, with 15 municipalities, while [Minas Gerais](/wiki/Minas_Gerais "Minas Gerais") is the most, with 853. Northern states are divided into small numbers of large municipalities (e.g. [Amazonas](/wiki/Amazonas_(Brazilian_state) "Amazonas (Brazilian state)") is divided into only 62 municipalities), and therefore they cover large areas incorporating several separated towns or villages that do not necessarily conform to one single conurbation. Southern and eastern states on the other hand, are divided into many small municipalities (e.g. [Minas Gerais](/wiki/Minas_Gerais "Minas Gerais")), and therefore large urban areas usually extend over several municipalities which form one single conurbation. More than a half of Brazilian municipalities have a population of lesser than 10,000 inhabitants.

The [Federal District](/wiki/Federal_District_(Brazil) "Federal District (Brazil)") cannot be divided into [municipalities](/wiki/Municipality "Municipality"), which is why its territory is composed of several [administrative regions](/wiki/Administrative_regions_of_the_Federal_District_(Brazil) "Administrative regions of the Federal District (Brazil)"). These regions are directly managed by the government of the Federal District, which exercises constitutional and legal powers that are equivalent to those of the [states](/wiki/Federated_state "Federated state"), as well as those of the municipalities, thus simultaneously assuming all the obligations arising from them.

The [1988 Brazilian Constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Brazil "Constitution of Brazil") treats the municipalities as parts of the Federation and not simply dependent subdivisions of the states. Each municipality has an autonomous local government, comprising a [mayor](/wiki/Mayor "Mayor") (*prefeito*) and a legislative body called municipal chamber (*[câmara municipal](/wiki/C%C3%A2mara_municipal "Câmara municipal")*). Both the local government and the legislative body are directly elected by the population every four years. These elections take place at the same time all over the country; the last [municipal elections](/wiki/2024_Brazilian_municipal_elections "2024 Brazilian municipal elections") were held on October 2024. Each municipality has the constitutional power to approve its own laws, as well as collecting taxes and receiving funds from the state and federal governments.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2) However, municipal governments have no judicial power [per se](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/per_se "wikt:per se"), and courts are only organised at the state or federal level. A subdivision of the state judiciary, or *[comarca](/wiki/Comarca "Comarca")*, can either correspond to an individual municipality or encompass several municipalities.

The seat of the municipal administration is a designated city (cidade), with no legal specifications regarding minimum population, area, or facilities. The city always shares the same name as the municipality, as they are not considered separate entities. Municipalities can be subdivided, solely for administrative purposes, into districts—often, new municipalities are formed from these districts. Other populated areas are villages, but these have no legal status or regulation. Almost all municipalities are subdivided into neighbourhoods (*[bairros](/wiki/Bairro "Bairro")*), although most municipalities do not officially define their neighbourhood limits (usually small cities in the [countryside](/wiki/Countryside "Countryside")).

Municipalities can be split or merged to form new municipalities within the borders of the state, if the population of the involved municipalities expresses a desire to do so in a [plebiscite](/wiki/Plebiscite "Plebiscite").[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2) However, these must abide by the [Brazilian Constitution](/wiki/Brazilian_Constitution "Brazilian Constitution"), and forming [exclaves](/wiki/Exclaves "Exclaves") or seceding from the state or union is expressly forbidden.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)
